sections.title: Stop These First: Common Myths
sections.summary: Two popular postpartum habits can actually make pelvic floor problems worse.
tips: Pause traditional 'belly breathing' where you simply push the belly out on the inhale — it increases intra-abdominal pressure and pushes the pelvic floor lower
Hold off on Kegels and ab workouts (crunches, planks) until you understand how your body has changed — rushing in can worsen things
Online workout routines aren't built for a postpartum body — blindly following them can increase abdominal pressure and aggravate pelvic floor issues
Learn the 360° breathing technique first — it's the safe foundation everything else builds on
warnings: Belly-only breathing after birth can aggravate leaking (incontinence) or organ prolapse
Premature core training can deepen diastasis recti (ab separation) instead of healing it
whenToCall: Persistent urine leakage when coughing, sneezing, or exercising
A feeling of heaviness, bulging, or pressure in the pelvis or vagina
A visible ridge or doming along the midline of your belly when sitting up — ask for a diastasis recti assessment
If any of these persist, ask for a referral to a pelvic floor physiotherapist

sections.title: How Pregnancy Changes Your Pelvis
sections.summary: The pelvis rotates outward and the pelvic floor stretches — this is why the belly pooch isn't just fat.
tips: During pregnancy the hip bones rotate outward, widening the bottom of the pelvis — this creates wider-looking hips, flatter glutes, and a thicker-looking waist
The sit bones separate and the pelvic outlet gets larger, which is normal but needs to be guided back
Birth stretches the ligaments and muscles of the pelvic floor, which can leave it lax and lower than before
A lax pelvic floor lets the organs sit lower, creating a protruding lower belly (a 'false pooch') that diet alone won't fix
Diastasis recti (separation of the abdominal muscles) is common and is part of the same picture
Good news: these are structural changes, and structure can be restored with the right sequence of training

sections.title: Rib Flare & Shallow Breathing
sections.summary: Pregnancy pushes the ribs out and up. If they stay flared, breathing stays shallow and pressure lands on the pelvic floor.
tips: As the belly grows, the ribs are pushed outward and upward ('rib flare')
The front of the ribcage gets compressed, so breathing becomes shallow and stays in the upper chest
If rib position isn't restored after birth, abdominal pressure can't be regulated properly
The organs then get squeezed downward with every breath, repeatedly loading the already weakened pelvic floor
Restoring chest expansion gives the organs room to move back up — this is why breathing work comes before ab work

sections.title: The Right Order: 3-Step Realignment
sections.summary: Good postpartum recovery is skeletal realignment, not just fat loss. Follow the sequence.
tips: Step 1 — Pelvis: restore the pelvis to a more closed, inwardly-rotated position to undo the outward flare
Step 2 — Ribcage: restore chest expansion and bring flared ribs back down, creating space for the organs to move up
Step 3 — Deep core: build deep core strength to hold the organs in their higher position and stabilise posture
The goal: ribcage and pelvis back to neutral — ribs not flared, pelvis not tipped forward
When the structure realigns, the waistline naturally slims and posture visibly improves — without crunches

sections.title: Core Technique: 360° Breathing
sections.summary: Expand the belly in all directions — sides, front AND back — instead of only pushing it forward.
tips: Inhale through the nose for about 3 seconds
Exhale slowly through the mouth for 6–8 seconds, like slowly blowing up a balloon
On the inhale, the abdomen should expand 360° — out to the left and right, forward, and into the back
This activates the diaphragm properly and regulates abdominal pressure instead of spiking it
Done well, it helps the organs move back up toward their original position
Repeat in relaxed cycles — a few minutes daily beats one long session
warnings: Traditional belly breathing (belly only pushes forward) increases pressure on the pelvic floor and can worsen organ descent — the 360° expansion is the whole point

sections.title: Exercise: Wall 90/90 Breathing
sections.summary: Lie on your back with hips and knees at 90°, feet flat on the wall — the foundational position for practising 360° breathing.
tips: Setup: lie on your back facing a wall, hips and knees both bent at 90°, with all three points of each foot pressed flat into the wall
Let the pelvis curl up slightly off the floor (a gentle tuck)
Inhale (nose, 3s): feel the chest lift toward the ceiling, and let the breath fill the chest, belly and back
Inhale: do NOT shrug the shoulders or use the neck, and don't deliberately push the belly forward
Exhale (mouth, 6–8s): blow out slowly like deflating a balloon, actively drawing the belly in
Exhale: feel the ribs knit inward and down while the chest relaxes back down
Advanced: reach the arms up toward the ceiling as you breathe — this increases front-of-chest expansion and helps correct rib flare
warnings: If you feel pelvic heaviness, pain, or you're holding your breath and straining, stop and rest — the breath should stay slow and quiet
